quiz.title: How HTTPS Works
quiz.subtitle: Secure web communication from basics to TLS internals
quiz.description: Test your understanding of HTTPS, TLS/SSL, certificates, handshakes, and secure communication on the web.

questions.text: What does HTTPS stand for?

options.text: HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure
HyperText Transport Protocol Standard
High Transfer Protocol Secure
HyperText Transmission Privacy System

questions.explanation: HTTPS stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure. It is HTTP with an added layer of encryption provided by TLS (Transport Layer Security).

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: There is no 'Standard' variant — the S stands for Secure, and the protocol is TLS, not 'Transport'.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: The H stands for HyperText, not High.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: HTTPS is not a privacy system acronym; it simply adds the word Secure to HTTP.

questions.text: On which default port does HTTPS operate?
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 by default. Plain HTTP uses port 80. Port 8080 is common for development servers and port 22 is SSH.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Port 80 is reserved for plain HTTP, not HTTPS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Port 8080 is an alternate HTTP port frequently used in development, not the HTTPS default.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Port 22 is used by SSH (Secure Shell), not HTTPS.

questions.text: Which protocol does HTTPS use to encrypt data?

options.text: SSH
FTP
TLS
IPSec

questions.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt the connection. SSL was the predecessor, but modern HTTPS uses TLS.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: SSH (Secure Shell) is a separate protocol used for secure remote login, not for web traffic encryption.
wrongAnswerFeedback.B: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is a file transfer protocol and is not an encryption layer.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: IPSec operates at the network layer and is typically used in VPNs, not in HTTPS.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of an SSL/TLS certificate?

options.text: To speed up page loading
To verify the server's identity and enable encryption
To compress website assets
To block malicious users

questions.explanation: An SSL/TLS certificate serves two purposes: it authenticates the server's identity (so the client knows who it is talking to) and it contains the public key needed to initiate encrypted communication.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Certificates have no effect on page load speed; they are for security, not performance.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Compression is handled separately (e.g., gzip/Brotli). Certificates are not involved.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Blocking malicious users is the role of firewalls and WAFs, not TLS certificates.

questions.text: What does the padlock icon in a browser's address bar indicate?

options.text: The website is completely safe and trustworthy
The connection between your browser and the server is encrypted
The website has no ads
Your password is saved for this site

questions.explanation: The padlock means the connection is encrypted with TLS. It does NOT mean the website itself is trustworthy or malware-free — phishing sites can also use HTTPS.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: HTTPS only means the connection is encrypted. A site can still be malicious even with a padlock.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: The padlock has nothing to do with advertisements.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Saved passwords are a browser feature unrelated to the TLS padlock.

questions.text: Who issues SSL/TLS certificates to websites?

options.text: Browser vendors like Google or Mozilla
Certificate Authorities (CAs)
Internet Service Providers (ISPs)
The website owner creates their own via the browser

questions.explanation: Certificate Authorities (CAs) are trusted organizations that issue and digitally sign TLS certificates. Examples include Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, and Comodo.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Browser vendors maintain a list of trusted CAs but do not issue certificates themselves.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: ISPs provide internet connectivity; they do not issue TLS certificates.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: While self-signed certificates exist, they are not issued by the browser and are not trusted by default.

questions.text: What type of encryption does TLS use for the actual data transfer after the handshake?

options.text: Asymmetric encryption
Symmetric encryption
Hashing
Base64 encoding

questions.explanation: After the TLS handshake establishes a shared session key (using asymmetric encryption), all subsequent data is encrypted with symmetric encryption because it is much faster.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Asymmetric encryption is used during the handshake to securely exchange keys, not for bulk data.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Hashing is a one-way operation used for integrity checks, not for encryption.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. It provides no confidentiality.

questions.text: What is the predecessor protocol to TLS?

options.text: SSH
SFTP
SSL
IPSec

questions.explanation: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) was the predecessor to TLS. All SSL versions (1.0, 2.0, 3.0) are now deprecated due to security vulnerabilities. TLS is the modern replacement.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: SSH is a separate secure protocol for remote shell access, not a predecessor to TLS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.B: SFTP is a file transfer protocol layered over SSH, not related to TLS ancestry.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: IPSec is a network-layer VPN protocol unrelated to TLS's lineage.

questions.text: If you visit a website over HTTP instead of HTTPS, what risk do you face?

options.text: The page loads slower
Data sent to and from the server can be read or modified by attackers on the network
The server cannot process your requests
Your IP address is revealed to the server

questions.explanation: HTTP sends all data in plain text. Anyone on the same network (e.g., a coffee shop Wi-Fi) can intercept and read or tamper with the traffic — a man-in-the-middle attack.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: HTTPS can actually add minor overhead, but HTTP is not meaningfully faster for security reasons.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: HTTP and HTTPS servers both process requests; protocol choice does not prevent server processing.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Your IP is visible to the server regardless of whether you use HTTP or HTTPS.

questions.text: What does a free certificate provider like Let's Encrypt offer?

options.text: Extended Validation (EV) certificates with company name in the browser
Free, automatically renewable Domain Validation (DV) certificates
Wildcard certificates for a one-time fee
Hardware Security Modules for certificate storage

questions.explanation: Let's Encrypt provides free Domain Validation (DV) certificates that can be automatically renewed every 90 days using the ACME protocol. It has dramatically increased HTTPS adoption.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: EV certificates require manual identity verification and are not offered free by Let's Encrypt.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Let's Encrypt does support wildcard certs but they are free, not for a one-time fee.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: HSMs are hardware devices; Let's Encrypt is a CA, not an HSM provider.

questions.text: During the TLS handshake, what is the purpose of the 'ClientHello' message?

options.text: The client sends its private key to the server
The client announces supported TLS versions, cipher suites, and a random value
The client requests the server's public key directly
The client sends its SSL certificate to the server

questions.explanation: ClientHello opens the TLS handshake. The client advertises the TLS versions it supports, the cipher suites it can use, and sends a random nonce that will later contribute to the session key derivation.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Private keys are never transmitted — doing so would completely undermine security.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: The public key comes from the server's certificate, sent in 'ServerHello'/'Certificate' messages.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Client certificates are optional and sent later in mutual TLS (mTLS), not in ClientHello.

questions.text: What is a cipher suite in the context of TLS?

options.text: A set of cryptographic algorithms used together for key exchange, authentication, encryption, and integrity
A list of trusted Certificate Authorities stored in the browser
The password used to unlock a private key
A firewall rule set for HTTPS traffic

questions.explanation: A cipher suite is a named combination of algorithms: one for key exchange (e.g., ECDHE), one for authentication (e.g., RSA), one for bulk encryption (e.g., AES-256-GCM), and one for integrity/MAC (e.g., SHA-384).

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Trusted CAs are stored in the OS/browser trust store, not called a cipher suite.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: A private key passphrase is unrelated to cipher suites.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Cipher suites are cryptographic specifications, not firewall or network rules.

questions.text: What is the role of asymmetric encryption during the TLS 1.2 handshake?

options.text: To encrypt all HTTP request bodies
To securely establish a shared secret that will be used for symmetric encryption
To compress data before sending
To replace the need for a Certificate Authority

questions.explanation: In TLS 1.2, asymmetric encryption (e.g., RSA or ECDHE) is used during the handshake to securely exchange a pre-master secret, from which a symmetric session key is derived for the actual data transfer.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: HTTP request bodies are encrypted with the symmetric session key, not asymmetric encryption.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: TLS does not perform compression; that is handled at the application or transport layer.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: CAs are still needed for certificate signing; asymmetric crypto does not replace them.

questions.text: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)?

options.text: A guarantee that no TLS handshake ever fails
A property ensuring that session keys cannot be derived from the server's long-term private key, so past sessions stay secure even if the private key is compromised
A technique for caching TLS sessions to avoid repeated handshakes
An algorithm that makes TLS certificates immune to expiry

questions.explanation: PFS (also called Forward Secrecy) means ephemeral key exchange algorithms (like ECDHE) generate a fresh key pair per session. Compromising the server's private key later cannot decrypt previously recorded sessions.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: PFS is a cryptographic property, not a guarantee about handshake reliability.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Session resumption/caching is a separate TLS feature (Session IDs / Session Tickets).
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: PFS has no effect on certificate expiry.

questions.text: What major change in TLS 1.3 reduced handshake latency compared to TLS 1.2?

options.text: TLS 1.3 removed certificate validation entirely
TLS 1.3 reduced the handshake to 1-RTT (and supports 0-RTT for resumption)
TLS 1.3 uses HTTP/2 exclusively
TLS 1.3 relies on the client to generate all encryption keys

questions.explanation: TLS 1.3 streamlined the handshake to require only 1 round-trip (1-RTT) versus 2 in TLS 1.2, and supports 0-RTT resumption for returning clients, significantly reducing connection latency.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Certificate validation is still required in TLS 1.3 — removing it would be insecure.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: TLS 1.3 is a transport security protocol and is independent of HTTP version.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Key exchange is still collaborative; TLS 1.3 uses ECDHE where both sides contribute.

questions.text: What is a Certificate Chain (Chain of Trust)?

options.text: A sequence of certificates linking the server certificate back to a trusted Root CA via intermediate CAs
A blockchain used to store certificate revocation data
A list of all active HTTPS connections in the browser
A hash of all certificates installed on a server

questions.explanation: Browsers trust a set of Root CAs. When a server sends its certificate, it also sends intermediate CA certificates forming a chain. The browser verifies the chain up to a trusted root to validate the server's identity.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Blockchain is not used in standard PKI certificate trust chains.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Active connections are tracked in browser internals, not in a certificate chain.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: A certificate chain is a hierarchy of signed certificates, not a digest of installed certs.

questions.text: What is Certificate Revocation, and which two mechanisms are commonly used?

options.text: The process of renewing a certificate; done via ACME and DNSSEC
The process of marking a certificate as no longer trusted before it expires; done via CRL and OCSP
The process of upgrading a DV certificate to EV; done via CAB Forum and PKCS#12
The process of pinning a certificate to a specific domain; done via HPKP and CSP

questions.explanation: If a private key is compromised or a certificate is mis-issued, the CA can revoke it. CRL (Certificate Revocation List) is a periodically published list; OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) allows real-time status checks.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Renewal is a separate process from revocation. ACME automates renewal but is unrelated to revocation checking.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Upgrading certificate types is a different process, not revocation.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: HPKP (HTTP Public Key Pinning) was a deprecated browser feature; it is not a revocation mechanism.

questions.text: What does HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) do?

options.text: It forces the server to use TLS 1.3 only
It tells the browser to always connect to the site over HTTPS and refuse plain HTTP connections for a specified duration
It encrypts cookies automatically
It pins the certificate public key in the browser cache

questions.explanation: HSTS is an HTTP response header (`Strict-Transport-Security`) that instructs browsers to only communicate with the site over HTTPS for `max-age` seconds, preventing protocol downgrade attacks.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: HSTS does not control the TLS version negotiated; that is up to the server's TLS configuration.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Cookie encryption is handled by the `Secure` flag on the cookie, not by HSTS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Key pinning was the purpose of HPKP, which is now deprecated. HSTS is different.

questions.text: What is SNI (Server Name Indication) and why is it important for HTTPS?

options.text: SNI is a TLS extension that allows a server to host multiple TLS certificates on the same IP address by sending the hostname at the start of the handshake
SNI is an HTTP/2 feature that multiplexes requests over a single connection
SNI is a DNS record type that maps a domain to its certificate fingerprint
SNI is a browser feature that caches the server's public key for future connections

questions.explanation: SNI is a TLS extension included in the ClientHello message. The client sends the target hostname so the server can select the correct certificate before the TLS handshake completes — essential for virtual hosting on shared IPs.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: HTTP/2 multiplexing is a separate feature at the HTTP layer, not a TLS extension.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: There is no DNS record type for certificate fingerprints in the standard SNI mechanism (TLSA records via DANE are different).
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: SNI operates at connection setup, not browser-side key caching.

questions.text: What is a man-in-the-middle (MitM) attack in the context of HTTPS, and how does certificate validation prevent it?

options.text: An attacker intercepts traffic by presenting a fake certificate; the browser detects this because the fake certificate cannot be validated back to a trusted CA
An attacker floods the server with TLS handshakes; prevented by rate limiting in HTTPS
An attacker reads encrypted packets; prevented by increasing key size
An attacker replaces the CA's root certificate, which HTTPS prevents by using public-key cryptography

questions.explanation: In a MitM attack the attacker tries to intercept HTTPS by presenting their own certificate. The browser rejects it because it cannot chain to a trusted Root CA. This is why certificate validation is fundamental to HTTPS security.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Flooding with handshakes is a DoS attack, not a classic MitM. HTTPS does not directly prevent DoS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: TLS already encrypts packets; simply reading encrypted ciphertext reveals nothing. MitM is about impersonating the server.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: An attacker cannot replace the CA root in the user's trusted store without system-level access; that is OS security, not HTTPS per se.
